concepts.title: Power
concepts.conceptBody:
POWER 

Power is the rate at which work is done, or the rate at which energy is used transferred.

Power \(=\frac{\text { work done }}{\text { time taken }}\)

The SI unit for power is the watt W.

A power of $1\text{ W}$ means that work is being done at the rate of $1\text{ J}/\text{s}$.

Larger units for power are the kilowatt $\text{kW}\left( 1\text{ kW}=1000\text{ W}={{10}^{3}}\text{ W} \right)$ and

the megawatt MW $\left( 1\text{MW}=1000000\text{ W}={{10}^{6}}\text{ W} \right).$

If work is being done by a machine moving at speed $\text{v}$ against a constant force, or resistance, $\text{F}$, then since work done is force times distance, work done per second is $\text{Fv}$, which is the same as power.

$\text{ Power }$ $=\text{Fv}$

instantenous power $=\left( \frac{\text{dW}}{\text{dt}} \right)=\frac{\vec{F}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}}{\text{dt}}=\left( \vec{F}\cdot \vec{V} \right)$

${{\text{P}}_{\text{instantaneous }}}$ $=\left( \vec{F}\cdot \vec{V} \right)$

$\text{ Average }\text{ power }$ $=\frac{\text{ total }\text{ work }\text{ done }}{\text{ total }\text{ time }}$

If force is constant

\(\begin{gathered}\langle\mathrm{P}\rangle=\frac{\int \mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{d} \vec{r}}{\int \mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{F} \frac{\Delta r}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{F} . \text { (average velocity) } \\ \langle\mathrm{P}\rangle=\mathrm{F} . \text { (avg. velocity) }\{\text {when F is constant only}\}\end{gathered}\)

An engine develops $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ of power. How much time will it take to lift a mass of $200 \mathrm{~kg}$ to a height of $40 \mathrm{~m}$. $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2\right)$

A car of mass $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ accelerates uniformly from rest to a velocity of $54 \mathrm{~km} /$ hour in $5 \mathrm{~s}$. The average power of the engine during this period in watts is (neglect friction)
